core_behavior: The system accepts an array of values and computes the arithmetic sum of all integer elements contained within it.
The result is a single numeric value representing the total.
An empty array produces an immediate result of zero without error.
The presence of any non-integer element halts processing and produces an error rather than a partial or coerced result.
inputs: A ordered collection of zero or more values of any type, passed as parameter `array` of type Array / List.
The array may contain values of mixed types (integers, floats, strings, booleans, nulls, nested arrays, objects, etc.).
The array itself must be a valid array/list structure. Passing a non-array (e.g., a scalar, object, or null) is treated as a distinct failure mode.
No maximum or minimum array length is specified beyond memory constraints.
outputs: When the array is empty, the return value is `0` of type Integer.
When the array contains one or more integers, the return value is the sum of all elements of type Integer.
The return type is always an integer on success, since the sum of integers is always an integer.
On error when any element is not an integer, the error message is: "Non-integer element encountered at index <i>: <value>".
On error when input is not an array, the error message is: "Input is not a valid array".
Errors include the index and value of the first offending element to aid debugging.
The system does not return a partial sum under any error condition.
assumptions: The system raises/throws an exception (or returns a structured error object in languages without exceptions) rather than returning a sentinel value such as `null` or `-1`.

An integer is defined as a whole number with no fractional component, matching the host language's native integer type (e.g., `int`, `long`). Values that are numerically whole but stored as floats (e.g., `3.0`) are not considered integers and will trigger an error. This is a strict type-check, not a value-check.
Booleans (`true`/`false`) are not treated as integers, even in languages where they are technically a subtype of integer (e.g., Python, C). A boolean element triggers an error.
`null` or `None` values are not integers and trigger an error.
Validation stops at the first non-integer element encountered (left to right, index 0 first). Only one error is reported per invocation.
A nested array is not an integer and triggers an error.
The input array is not modified during processing.
The implementation should traverse the array once, validating and summing simultaneously, rather than in two separate passes.
No shared mutable state is used; the function is stateless and safe to call concurrently.
failure_modes: Element is a float (e.g., `3.14`, `3.0`): Raise error: "Non-integer element encountered at index <i>: <value>".
Element is a string (e.g., `"5"`): Raise error: "Non-integer element encountered at index <i>: <value>".
Element is a boolean (`true`/`false`): Raise error: "Non-integer element encountered at index <i>: <value>".
Input is not a valid array (e.g., a scalar, object, or null): Raise error: "Input is not a valid array".
edge_cases: An empty array produces an immediate result of zero without error.
Values that are numerically whole but stored as floats (e.g., `3.0`) are not considered integers and will trigger an error.
Booleans are not treated as integers even in languages where they are technically a subtype of integer.
performance_constraints: No maximum or minimum array length is specified beyond memory constraints.
The implementation should traverse the array once, validating and summing simultaneously, rather than in two separate passes.
No shared mutable state is used; the function is stateless and safe to call concurrently.
